result.markdown:
--- title: "Interactive Session 2B" subtitle: "Higher order derivatives, partial derivatives, plotting functions in ggplot" editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ## 1. Partial derivatives by hand a. Algal biomass ($B$, grams) in a system is modeled by the following, where $h$ is time (hours) and $T$ is temperature (Celsius). $$B(h, T)=0.6Th+2.1h^2$$ For water at 28 degrees, find an expression for how biomass is changing with respect to time ($\frac{\partial B}{\partial h}$). ::: {.callout-tip collapse=true} ## Solution $\frac{\partial B}{\partial h}=0.6T + (2)(2.1)h$ $\frac{\partial B}{\partial h}=(0.6)(28) + 4.2h$ $\frac{\partial B}{\partial h}=16.8 + 4.2h$ ::: ## 2. Plotting a function using `{ggplot2}` - Add a new Quarto doc (I'm saving mine as `day2b-exercises.qmd`) to your existing `eds212-day2-GHpractice` repository and follow along with the exercises, below. #### Example 1: ::: {.callout-tip collapse=true} ## Use keyboard shortcuts to create a function in R In the code chunk below, we create an R function to plot the function, $f(x) = 3x^2 + 4$. We can use a nifty keyboard shortcut to create a R function around this expression following these steps: 1. Write out the expression $3x^2 + 4$ in a code chunk: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} 3 * x^2 + 4 ``` ::: 2. Highlight the expression, then use the keyboard shortcut `control` + `option` + `X` (Mac) or `Ctrl` + `Alt` + `X` (Windows) to pop open a dialog box, which will prompt you to provide a name for your function (you can call it `eq1`). 3. Watch your function appear! The shortcut will automatically add any variables from the body of your function (here, just `x`) as arguments inside `function()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} eq1 <- function(x) { 3 * x^2 + 4 } ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # load packages ---- library(tidyverse) # Define function ---- eq1 <- function(x) { 3 * x^2 + 4 } # create data frame with range of values to evaluate function over ---- my_data_range <- data.frame(x = c(1, 100)) # Plot it as a continuous curve over a specific range using `geom_function()` ---- # `geom_function()` automatically evaluates the function over the range of x-values specified in the df and adds the resulting y-values to the plot ggplot(data = my_data_range, aes(x = x)) + geom_function(fun = eq1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](interactive-session-2b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse=true} ## Additional practice #### Example 2: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Define function ---- eq2 <- function(x) { 2.4 - 5*x^3 + 2.1*x^2 } # Plot it as a continuous curve over a specific range using `geom_function()` (here, we define our data frame directly inside `ggplot()` ---- ggplot(data = data.frame(x = c(-50, 50)), aes(x = x)) + geom_function(fun = eq2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](interactive-session-2b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: ## 3. Plotting derivatives Let's look at the function: $C(t)=t^3$ Let's plot the function along with the first derivative: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Store the function C(t) ---- ct <- function(t) { t^3 } # create data frame with range of values to evaluate function over ---- my_data_range <- data.frame(t = c(-5, 5)) # Plot it ---- ggplot(data = my_data_range, aes(x = t)) + geom_function(fun = ct) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](interactive-session-2b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Find the derivative ---- dc_dt <- D(expr = expression(t^3), name = "t") dc_dt ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 3 * t^2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Store the derivative as a function ---- dc_dt_fun <- function(t) { 3 * t ^2 } # Then plot them together ---- ggplot(data = my_data_range, aes(x = t)) + geom_function(fun = ct, color = "red") + geom_function(fun = dc_dt_fun, color = "blue") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](interactive-session-2b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-2.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## 4. Partial derivatives in R Specify the variable you want to take the derivative with respect to. $$f(x,y,z)=2xy-3x^2z^3$$ Find partial, $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}$: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create the expression ---- f_xyz <- expression(2 * x * y - 3 * x^2 * z^3) # Partial with respect to x ---- df_dx <- D(expr = f_xyz, name = "x") df_dx ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 2 * y - 3 * (2 * x) * z^3 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse=true} ## Additional practice Find partials, $\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ and $\frac{\partial f}{\partial z}$: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Partial with respect to y ---- df_dy <- D(expr = f_xyz, name = "y") df_dy ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 2 * x ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Partial with respect to z ---- df_dz <- D(expr = f_xyz, name = "z") df_dz ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` -(3 * x^2 * (3 * z^2)) ``` ::: ::: ::: ## 5. Stage, commit, (pull) and push your changes to GitHub Follow the same pattern that we practiced in today's earlier [interactive session (2A)](interactive-session-2a.qmd#hello-git-github), and verify that it worked by checking out your GitHub repo.
::: {.center-text .body-text-xl .teal-text} End interactive session 2B :::
